content:
From Infrastructure to Intelligence
Understanding the Architecture Layers of a Cloud-Native 5G Core
When people hear 5G Core (5GC), they often think about network functions like AMF, SMF, or UPF.
But behind every successful 5G deployment lies a carefully layered cloud-native architecture. Each layer has a distinct responsibility, and together they transform telecom networks into scalable, resilient, software-defined platforms.
As AI begins interacting directly with operational systems through protocols like Model Context Protocol (MCP), understanding these layers becomes even more important.
Let's explore the stack—from the infrastructure powering everything to the intelligence layer sitting on top.
---
Layer 1: Infrastructure
Every cloud-native application starts with compute.
This layer provides the physical or virtual resources required to run workloads.
It includes:
- Physical servers
- Virtual Machines
- Public cloud
- Private cloud
- Hybrid cloud
- Storage
- Networking
- Security
Without this foundation, none of the higher layers can exist.
Think of it as the land on which an entire city is built.
---
Layer 2: Container Runtime
Traditional applications depended heavily on operating systems.
Cloud-native applications package everything they need into containers.
The container runtime is responsible for:
- Running containers
- Isolating workloads
- Managing container lifecycle
- Ensuring portability across environments
Common runtimes include:
- containerd
- CRI-O
- Docker Engine (primarily for development)
In telecom, every Network Function (NF) can run inside its own container.
---
Layer 3: Kubernetes
If containers are individual buildings,
Kubernetes is the city planner.
Running hundreds of containers manually would be impossible.
Kubernetes automates:
- Scheduling
- Scaling
- Self-healing
- Rolling updates
- Service discovery
- Configuration management
- Secret management
- Load balancing
When a pod crashes,
Kubernetes creates another.
When traffic increases,
Kubernetes scales automatically.
This orchestration layer makes telecom networks resilient.
---
Layer 4: Helm
Imagine deploying 40 Kubernetes resources manually.
Now imagine deploying them with one command.
That's Helm.
Helm acts as the package manager for Kubernetes.
Instead of writing dozens of YAML files every time,
Helm bundles them into reusable Charts.
Benefits include:
- Version-controlled deployments
- Rollbacks
- Configuration reuse
- Simplified upgrades
- Consistent deployments across environments
Most production telecom applications today rely heavily on Helm Charts.
---
Layer 5: Database Layer (MongoDB)
A telecom core processes enormous amounts of information.
Subscriber profiles.
Policies.
Authentication data.
Charging information.
Session context.
Configuration.
A cloud-native database stores this information efficiently.
MongoDB is frequently used because it offers:
- Horizontal scalability
- High availability
- Flexible document model
- Fast access for cloud-native applications
The database isn't merely storage.
It becomes the memory of the network.
---
Layer 6: 5G Core Network Functions
This is where telecom intelligence begins.
Each Network Function has one responsibility.
Examples include:
AMF – Registration and mobility management
SMF – Session management
UPF – User plane packet forwarding
UDM – Subscriber management
AUSF – Authentication
PCF – Policy control
NRF – Service discovery
NEF – Secure exposure of network capabilities
BSF – Binding Support Function
Unlike legacy telecom systems,
these functions are independent microservices.
They can scale independently.
They can be upgraded independently.
They communicate using APIs instead of proprietary interfaces.
---
Layer 7: Service-Based Architecture (SBA)
This is one of the biggest architectural shifts introduced in 5G.
Instead of tightly coupled interfaces,
Network Functions expose RESTful services over HTTP/2.
Benefits include:
- Loose coupling
- Easier integration
- Dynamic service discovery
- Better scalability
- Faster innovation
The NRF enables Network Functions to discover each other dynamically.
This makes the architecture much more flexible than previous generations.
---
Layer 8: MCP (Model Context Protocol)
This is where AI enters the picture.
MCP is not part of the 3GPP 5G Core architecture. Instead, it can be viewed as an integration layer that sits above the cloud-native stack, allowing AI assistants and applications to interact with enterprise systems through a standardized protocol.
Rather than building custom integrations for every database, API, or tool, an AI application can communicate through MCP servers that expose these capabilities in a consistent way.
In a telecom environment, this opens up possibilities such as:
- Retrieving Kubernetes pod status through natural language.
- Querying MongoDB for subscriber or session information (with appropriate authorization).
- Accessing logs and metrics for troubleshooting.
- Triggering Helm deployments or rollbacks.
- Correlating events across multiple network functions.
- Assisting engineers during incident analysis.
Importantly, MCP does not replace Kubernetes, Helm, MongoDB, or the 5G Core itself. It provides a standardized interface that AI systems can use to access these existing components securely and consistently.
As AI assistants become part of engineering workflows, protocols like MCP may become an important bridge between human intent and operational systems.
---
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Putting It All Together
Here's how the stack fits together:
- Infrastructure provides compute, storage, networking, and security.
- Container Runtime runs the individual workloads.
- Kubernetes orchestrates and manages those workloads.
- Helm packages and deploys applications consistently.
- MongoDB stores operational and subscriber data.
- 5G Core Network Functions deliver telecom services.
- Service-Based Architecture enables communication between network functions using APIs.
- MCP can sit above the stack, enabling AI applications to interact with these systems through a common protocol.
Every layer has a specific responsibility.
Remove one layer, and the system becomes harder to deploy, operate, or scale.
---
Final Thoughts
The telecom industry is no longer just about protocols and packet forwarding.
It is increasingly shaped by cloud-native principles, automation, and AI.
Understanding the architecture layer by layer makes it easier to troubleshoot issues, design resilient deployments, and appreciate how modern 5G networks are assembled.
For me, this exploration is more than learning another technology. It's about connecting cloud infrastructure, telecom architecture, and AI into one cohesive picture.
The future of telecom won't just be cloud-native.
It will be cloud-native, programmable, and increasingly AI-assisted.
